source.title: Hard Decisions Shape the Neural Coding of Preferences

confidence_rationale: fMRI + eye-tracking study with the directly architecturally-load-bearing finding: choice-induced preference change becomes BEHAVIOURALLY RELEVANT only for choices that were REMEMBERED, and remembering is associated with hippocampus activity. This is the cleanest available empirical link between commit-record (hippocampus) and downstream belief revision cost. dlPFC and precuneus predict preference change online during decision-making. Source quality high (J Neurosci, fMRI + eye-tracking + memory probe). Mapping fidelity is the highest of any entry in the pull because the experimental design directly probes the architectural prediction: post-commit representational shift requires the agent to retain the record of having committed.
failure_signatures: Choices that are NOT remembered do NOT produce behaviourally relevant preference shifts -- meaning the lock requires the commit record. This is precisely the prediction of the commit-boundary mechanism. If the lock occurred independently of the commit record, the proposed mechanism would be wrong.

mapping.source_claim_statement: In hard decisions between equally valued alternatives, preferences are updated online during decision-making, with the magnitude of update predicted by left dlPFC and precuneus activity. The behavioural relevance of these preference adjustments depends on whether the choice was subsequently remembered, and remembering is associated with hippocampus activity.
mapping.ree_translation: Direct architectural anchor for the brief. The proposed commit-boundary belief lock mechanism predicts that revision cost asymmetry depends on the agent's record of having committed -- if the commit record is intact, the belief is locked; if the record is lost, the lock dissolves. Voigt's design isolates exactly this dependency: same choice, same dissonance, same dlPFC engagement -- but no behavioural consequence unless the hippocampus retained the record of the choice. This is the strongest empirical match in the pull for the architectural prediction.
mapping.mapping_caveat: The paradigm tests preference change over consumer items, not propositional belief revision under counter-evidence. The 'remembered vs not' contrast is between same-day forgetting of low-stakes choices and recall of low-stakes choices, not between high-stakes acted-upon beliefs and unacted-upon beliefs. So the architectural prediction is tested in a low-stakes valuation context; transferring to clinical delusion-resistance scenarios is one inferential step.
mapping.source_context: Healthy adults; fMRI with eye-tracking; food-item preference paradigm; subsequent memory probe; Australia.

confidence_components.notes: J Neurosci, methodologically clean (fMRI + eye-tracking + behaviour + memory probe). Mapping fidelity is unusually high because the experimental dissociation (remembered vs forgotten choices) IS the architectural prediction. Transfer risk modest -- consumer preferences vs clinical beliefs.
